A detailed cost analysis for customers can be found in this blog post. 

Recommended System Landscapes: Finding the Right Fit 

The various landscapes are only briefly explained here, detailed descriptions can be found in the documentation. When setting up the systems, we recommend that you only start with the system that is currently required. It is not necessary to set up the entire system landscape directly. It is sufficient if only the development system is set up first and then the test or production system as soon as the need is met. On the one hand, this provides a better overview at the beginning and on the other hand, costs can be reduced. 

3-System Landscape 

This landscape consists of a development system, a quality assurance system and the production system. This allows the tests of new developments to be tested directly in parallel and ensures that the application is also executable in non-development systems. 

5 System Landscape 

In this scenario, the code lines for development and corrections can be handled separately. The correction system and the test system are on the same level as the production system. This means that the respective corrections can be tested before they are imported into the production system. To develop larger releases and code parts, there is an actual development system. There is also the corresponding test system. Urgent corrections can be implemented directly and have no influence on the normal development cycle. 

Note: At this point you can use the system hibernation function, because if it is not necessary to enter corrections frequently, the correction system and the associated test system can be shut down and thus also save costs. If there is a need for these systems, they can simply be started up again. A detailed explanation of the system hibernation can be found in this blog post. 

This learning journey  can give you an overview of how to create an ABAP environment on the SAP BTP or you can attend our monthly Customer and Partner Roundtable. Among other topics, new product features are presented in this meeting, live demos are shown by experts or you have the opportunity to ask questions live. To register for the roundtable, please follow the SAP Community for SAP BTP ABAP Environment, where the next date will be announced in a blog post and registration details will be provided. If you have missed a roundtable or would like to look at past ones again, you can find them on GitHub, where the respective slides are also available. 

Hopefully this blog post has given you a clear introduction to the SAP BTP ABAP environment.

entry.title: Santa's Early Gift for SAP BTP
entry.content:
At this time of year, as many of us are looking forward to some fun over the festive period, I like many people am busy wrapping presents for friends and family. But in the fine spirit of giving, the rather nice team at G2, playing the role of Santa in their smart red and white livery, have given the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) team an early Christmas gift, in the form of Winter 2025 badges, celebrating SAP BTP’s recognition as a Leader across a number of different categories in the G2 Winter 2025 reports. This includes a Momentum badge this time around too, indicating that our customers place us in the top 25% of leaders in certain product categories. What a nice surprise for the team, and a great way to round out 2024!

The recognition in new G2 reports builds on that received just a few short months ago in their Fall 2024 reports, and shows a continued appreciation from SAP’s customers for the value they derive from using SAP BTP, as badges are offered only following a robust scoring methodology which ranks vendor solutions based on real, verified, and trusted software buyers’ reviews. This of course gives the SAP BTP team a great feeling of pride, and gratitude too, knowing that our customers see us in this way. And, I know I express this for the whole team, when I say “thank you” to the SAP customers who left reviews with G2 that enabled us to achieve this recognition.

Where are customers feeling the value from SAP BTP?

Of course, SAP customers use SAP BTP for a variety of business needs, and so the value they feel from the platform and services, flows to them in a number of different ways. Some of this value is described in the reviews that customers leave at the SAP BTP landing page on the G2 website. Here are a few examples:

“It effortlessly connects different systems and makes everything work together smoothly. It also gives you the tools to get creative, build what you need and adapt as your business grows…flexibility for implementation is superb and the support from SAP makes it better because of which it’s very easy and convenient.”

(User review by a mid-market customer, Dec 2024)

 

“The feature I appreciate the most in SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) is its multifunctionality and integration. It is possible to optimize business processes through a single platform which integrates several SAP and third-party systems together. Thanks to the cloud-based business services offered on the Platform, along with powerful analytics and ML capabilities, it is an excellent environment for building and releasing out-of-the-box solutions. And finally, the low-code/no-code environment allows business users themselves to create the required solutions and thus speed up digital change.”

(User review by small business customer, Sept 2024)

 

“SAP business technology is very User friendly & is an end to end solution…It basically solved all my procurement transformation needs.”

(User review by enterprise customer, Dec 2024)

So, as 2024 draws to a close, I’d like to thank both G2 for recognizing SAP BTP as a Leader in many of the 2024 reports as well as the new Winter 2025 reports, and of course thanks to our customers too, for all of the continued kind words and support, and for choosing SAP BTP as the platform to help run your business.

Thanks for a super end to 2024, and here’s to a wonderful 2025 ahead!

Chris Grundy, Product Marketing, SAP BTP

entry.title: Wrapping up 2024 for Application Development, Automation and Integration on SAP BTP: What a year!
entry.content:
2024 has been an incredibly busy year for our application development, automation and integration portfolio on the SAP Business Technology Platform. As the year draws to a close, I wanted to take some time to reflect on our achievements. Indeed, I am very grateful to the people in my teams who have worked very hard to deliver so many improvements to our solutions, and I would like to say a big thank you to all of them.

Of course, you've heard a lot about Joule, our AI co-pilot, over the course of the year. In fact, we have been integrating AI in general, and Generative AI with Joule in particular, into all components of our portfolio, as we announced earlier. And we demonstrated our AI capabilities at SAP TechEd two months ago. This deployment of Business AI has generated great interest from our customers and partners, as well as analysts.

But we've also introduced many new features and enhancements, which I'm proud to present in detail below.

SAP Build

In terms of application development and automation, in addition to embedding AI in all of our solutions, our biggest improvement in 2024 was to enrich SAP Build with pro-code capabilities. Indeed, we announced SAP Build Code at SAP TechEd 2023, made available in March this year, and we recently added ABAP Environments to SAP Build. SAP Build now allows business users and developers to work across their low-code, Java/JavaScript and ABAP projects from a unique starting point, the SAP Build lobby, benefitting of seamless interoperability capabilities fostering fusion development.

With this, SAP Build is now THE extensibility solution for any SAP customer: you can now accelerate your ERP extension development and modernization, foster innovation, and automate processes, all in a single, comprehensive suite of AI-driven tools for extension development. And SAP S/4HANA users can create extensions with SAP Build directly from the ERP business application, thanks to our new Extensibility Wizard for S/4HANA Cloud Public Edition.

This year, we have worked hard to improve the integration of the SAP Build components, both with other components of SAP BTP and with other SAP solutions: for example, SAP Build Process Automation is now integrated with SAP Build Apps and also with Advanced Event Mesh, applications created with SAP Build Apps can be deployed in SAP Build Work Zone, and SAP Build Work Zone supports multi-tenancy.

Finally, I would like to mention that we have improved the usability of SAP Build, for example with a new process layout and script task editor in SAP Build Process Automation and guided experiences in SAP Build Work Zone.

This image summarizes all the improvements we delivered this year:

A few weeks ago, Gartner has recognized SAP as a Visionary in its 2024 Magic Quadrant for Enterprise Low-Code Application Platforms (LCAP). I would like to thank the SAP Build team for achieving this great result!

You can find more details in these blog posts from our product experts:

  • Product Updates for SAP Build Code – November 2024
  • Product Updates for SAP Build Code – October 2024
  • What’s New in SAP Build – Q3 2024 Edition
  • Product Updates for SAP Build Code – September 2024
  • Product Updates for SAP Build Code – July 2024
  • What’s New in SAP Build – Q2 2024 Edition
  • What’s New in SAP Build – Seamless Integration of Processes and Apps
  • What’s New with SAP Build Process Automation: January 2024

SAP Integration Suite

In 2024 we have also improved SAP Integration Suite in many different areas:

Regarding SAP PI/PO migration, which is a topic on which I published a blog post earlier this year to explain Advanced Event Mesh and Edge Integration Cell, we introduced a Pipeline concept and new rules added for migration assessment.

Edge Integration Cell is now available on Google Cloud and Red Hat OpenShift. It supports outgoing connections through a HTTP proxy and provides offline availability during periods of temporary connectivity loss.

This year, Event Mesh became a capability of SAP Integration Suite, which also allows direct consumption of SAP S/4HANA Cloud Events and distributed tracing, from sending to receiving applications across one or multiple event brokers.

We have been investing heavily in improving developer efficiency and operational excellence. A few of Gen AI Increments that we recently launched were to accelerate development of integration flows with Gen AI and analyze APIs using API Anomaly detection in API Management

You can also accelerate connectivity with SAP Integration Suite using new adapters for Coupa, NetSuiteERP, SnowFlake, and Amazon DynamoDB, SMB, Jira, Amazon EventBridge, HubSpot, Anaplan with many more planned to be released soon.

We enhanced Cloud Integration and API Management user experience with SAP Horizon theme. Enriched the Cloud Integration capability with the ability to inspect content size for all the integration artifacts, manage access at the package level, validate integration flow design as per the guidelines in integration flow editor.

Furthermore, API Management now supports multiple target endpoints in API proxy tooling and recently launched Developer Hub for search, discovery and consumption of APIs. We also launched oData provisioning as a new capability.

We have onboarded 25+ partners with a contract and further enriched our marketplace with 53+ new partner content in particular 7 new APIs, 4 Processes, 20+ Integration packages and 22+ Validated partner use cases added on SAP Business Accelerator Hub.

Finally, B2B Scenarios for Trading Partner Management (TPM) and Integration Advisor have also been enriched with functionalities like archiving of Payload data for Trading Partner Management scenarios, EDI flat file payloads can be directly used to simulate mappings, for easier access to envelope fields these can be added in the Integration Advisor, simplified mass-maintenance in TPM, support for custom SOA messages in Integration Advisor, new Tradacoms message standard.

You can find more details in these blog posts from our product experts:

  • Whats New For SAP Integration Suite – August 2024
  • What’s New for SAP Integration Suite – June & July 2024
  • What’s New for SAP Integration Suite – Spring 2024
  • What’s New for SAP Integration Suite – February 2024
  • What's New for SAP Integration Suite - January 2024

Feel free to follow our #WhatsNewInSAPIntegrationSuite on SAP Community and register for our webinar for updates on upcoming innovations!

Finally, I’m also proud to share that with SAP Integration Suite SAP has been named a Leader for 4th consecutive time by Gartner® in its 2024 Magic Quadrant™ for iPaaS. In addition, SAP is a Leader in IDC MarketScape: Worldwide Business-to-Business Middleware 2024 Vendor Assessment.

Kudos to the team for these achievements!

What’s for 2025?

As you’ve seen above, we delivered a lot of new features and enhancements in 2024. And for sure we will continue in 2025!

You can find out what we have planned for next year on our public roadmap for application development, automation and integration on the SAP Business Technology Platform.

With that, I wish you a Merry Christmas and very happy holidays!  🎅🏻🎄

entry.title: NOW RELEASED: Upload and work with external files in SAP Cloud ALM for implementation
entry.content:
SAP Cloud ALM streamlines application lifecycle management for SAP customers by combining efficiency and cost-effectiveness. With its automatic updates and easy setup, it eliminates the need for hardware investments, complex configurations, or technical upgrades. Best of all, SAP Cloud ALM is free of subscription fees. Under SAP’s fair usage policy, customers benefit from built-in resources, including 8 GB of SAP HANA memory and monthly outbound API data transfers, allowing businesses to focus on growth while minimizing technical overhead.

Previously, SAP Cloud ALM users could only reference external files stored in disparate locations using URLs. Given the widespread use of Word documents, PDFs, Excel sheets, and similar formats, managing documents efficiently was a challenge.

We are excited to announce that it is now possible to upload documents directly into SAP Cloud ALM for implementation through an integration with SAP BTP Document Management Service (DMS).

About SAP BTP Document Management Service (DMS)

SAP BTP Document Management Service is a cloud-based solution for secure and scalable document storage and management. It enables businesses to seamlessly store, manage, and share files across various SAP applications and processes. This integration aligns with SAP's strategy to provide harmonized and centralized document management capabilities.

More details about SAP BTP DMS can be found here:
Document Management Service Documentation 

How to Connect SAP Cloud ALM to SAP BTP DMS

To store external files in SAP Cloud ALM, customers must request:

  1. SAP Document Management Service, Integration Option
  2. SAP Document Management Service, Repository Option

Once these services are requested, a connection between SAP Cloud ALM and SAP BTP DMS can be established, enabling seamless document uploads and management. More details on connecting the service can be found in the Set up & Admin Guide of SAP Cloud ALM.

For SAP BTP DMS there are different Service plans available.

Documents managed via SAP Cloud ALM are stored in a dedicated repository. These documents can be uploaded, replaced, or deleted exclusively through SAP Cloud ALM, ensuring proper change tracking and preparing for future enhancements, such as versioning and document history.

Core Functionalities in the First Release

The initial version introduces essential capabilities:

1. Uploading External Files

Once the integration with SAP BTP Document Management Service (DMS) is established, users can upload one file per document in SAP Cloud ALM. To do this:

  • Create a document in SAP Cloud ALM.

  • Access the "External File" tab, where you can upload a file (maximum size: 10 MB) stored on your PC or another location. Supported MIME types can be checked in the SAP Cloud ALM application help.
  • Click “Upload” to store the file in the SAP BTP DMS repository. The repository operates seamlessly in the background, invisible to end-users.

  • The file name and size will appear in the External File tab. A draft version of the document is automatically created. If the draft is discarded, the file is also deleted. To finalize, click “Create” to save the document.

Uploaded documents are fully manageable within SAP Cloud ALM: users can assign a document type, owner, status, tags, and relate the document to processes or other elements.

In the list view, documents with external files appear alongside others, and new filters enable searching specifically for these documents.

2. Replacing External Files

To update a file:

  • Download the current version via the provided link.
  • Edit locally and use the "Replace" option while in edit mode to upload the new version.

If working in draft mode, the saved version remains accessible until the changes are finalized.

3. Searching and Copying Documents

File names are searchable from the list view, even when they differ from the document title.

Additionally, documents with external files can be copied, creating a new version in the repository that can be modified independently.

4. Deleting External Files

To delete an external file:

  • Switch to edit mode and select “Delete”. Only the file in the DMS repository is removed, while the document remains.
  • To delete both the document and its external file, save changes and delete the document in the standard way. Mass deletions can also be performed from the list view.

Looking Ahead

The first release marks the beginning of capabilities in SAP Cloud ALM working with external files. Planned improvements include:

  • Document Previews
  • Versioning and Document History
  • Transfer of Documents from SAP Solution Manager

Your feedback is very welcome — please share your ideas, questions, and influence requests to help shape the future of SAP Cloud ALM.

entry.title: SAP Build Apps - upload files to SAP Document Information Extraction (DOX) Service with camera
entry.content:
Introduction

Uploading files is a common requirement in various business scenarios. As SAP Build Apps developer, we are often required to add file upload capability into our apps to fulfill business requirements and enhance user experience.

One of such use case is to upload documents to SAP Document Information Extraction (DOX) Service to process large business documents that have content in headers and tables. You can use the extracted information, for example, to automatically process payables, invoices, or payment notes and make sure that invoices and payables match.

Its Upload Document API requires you to sent a POST request with "multipart/form-data" payload.

In this blog, I will show how you can make use of the new "HTTP Destination Request" flow function along with "Take photo" flow function to achieve that.

By the end of this blog, you will learn how to:

  1. Set up your DOX service destination to work with Build Apps.
  2. Install your destination to be used in Build Apps and test the connection to your destination in Build Apps.
  3. Use "Take photo" to let user to capture photo with device and pass the resulting image to "HTTP destination request"
  4. Use "HTTP destination request" to upload the image to your DOX service API via the installed destination.

Pre-requisites

  1. You must have SAP Build Apps already subscribed in your subaccount.
  2. You must have SAP Document Information Extraction Service subscribed.
  3. All required roles for SAP Builds Apps and Document Information Extraction Service are assigned accordingly to your user.

Setting up Destination for Document Information Extraction Service

If you already have a working destination to your DOX service, you can use it or clone it while referring to the steps below to make it compatible with the examples in this blog:

  1. Go to SAP BTP cockpit and create a destination pointing to your DOX instance.
    • For this example, I recommend that the URL of the destination is pointing to the root of your DOX service instance:
      • Example: https://aiservices-dox.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/document-information-extraction/v1/
      • We will append additional relative path to your destination later when using the "HTTP Destination Request" flow function.
    • For authentication related configurations, you can refers to your DOX instance's service key file. These configuration are inside the "uaa" section of the file.
      • TIPS: Token Service URL typically ends with /oauth/token. The UAA URL you copied from the service key usually doesn't contain that. So, you must append /oauth/token yourself when entering the URL to the Token Service URL field.
    • Add the required additional properties in the destination for Build Apps' usage:
      • HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
      • BuildApps.Enabled: true
      • BuildApps.ApiType: rest
      • MobileEnabled: true
    • These are the minimal properties required for Build Apps, you may add other properties for other purposes too. Example:

      Hint: "Check Connection" in the BTP Destination cockpit only tells you that the URL is reachable, it does not indicate that the authentication is set up correctly. So, you should not rely solely on it to tell you that your destination is configured correctly.

Install DOX Service Destination in Your Build Apps Project

  1. Open your Build Apps project.
  2. Go to “Integrations” tab.
    1. Press on “Add Integration” button.
    2. Press on the “BTP DESTINATIONS” button in the SAP Systems card.
    3. Search for your DOX destination and select it. It should show a screen with “The destination was recognised as REST api” message.

      Note:
      • If you can't find your destination, do check that you have added the BuildApps.Enabled=true property in your destination.
      • If you don't see that message,do check that you have added the BuildApps.ApiType=rest property in your destination.
    4. Press "INSTALL INTEGRATION" button to install the destination.
    5. (Optional) Testing the connection to destination using Entity's list operation
      1. You can test the connection to your DOX destination by creating adding a REST API data entity and use it to call one of the GET API available in your service, assuming you have the right access. Otherwise, you can skip this steps or choose different API to test with.
      2. Press on the "Add REST API Data Entity" button. You should get a dialog pop up.
      3. In the dialog, enter the following information:
        • Name: Documents
        • Autogenerate configuration: enabled
        • Entity path within the API: /document/jobs
        • Enable Capabilities: enable "list" and uncheck the rest of them
        • Press "Add" button
      4. You should now see the DocumentJobs entity and its Fields and more.
        If you are seeing error here and it couldn't generate any Fields for you, then it's likely there's something wrong with your path, or destination configuration. Make sure to check your destination's URL and authentication configuration.
      5. You can use "Browse Real Data" button to check if you can see expected data entries and no errors.

      6. If you are not seeing any errors, it means the connection to your destination is good for usage.
    6. You can now Exit this page.

Using HTTP Destination Request to Upload File

  1. Go to “User Interface” tab.
  2. Press on the button (or any other UI control) that you wish to use to trigger the file upload and then press on the "Add logic to <your control>" at the bottom of the screen. This should show the Logic Canvas.
  3. Press the “Marketplace” button under the "Core" tab on the left panel.
    1. Search for “HTTP destination request” flow function and install it.
    2. After it is installed you should see it in the "INSTALLED" tab of the Logic Canvas.
  4. You are now ready to use “HTTP destination request” to upload file:
    1. From the "CORE" tab, scroll down to the "Device" category and drag “Take Photos” flow function to the logic flow canvas.
      ℹ️ Note: "Take photo" flow function is currently only available when you run your app in mobile device. It will do nothing when triggered as web app.
    2. Link the button's “Event: Component tap” node to the “Take photo” node.
    3. Go to the "INSTALLED" tab and drag “HTTP destination request” to the logic flow canvas.
    4. Link “Take photo” node’s output port 1 to the “HTTP destination request” node, because we want to pass the photo taken by user to the “HTTP destination request” node.
    5. Click on the “HTTP destination request” node and on its property panel.
      1. In the Destination dropdown select your installed destination.
      2. Set the “HTTP method” as “POST”.
      3. Set the “Request body type” as “multipart/form-data”.
      4. Set the “Request body” and add entries for each part needed for the target API. In this case, we will be using the SAP Document Information Extraction Upload Document API which requires 2 properties:
        KeyValue
        optionsBind this value to formula using the following sample: ENCODE_JSON({"schemaName":"SAP_invoice_schema","clientId": "default","documentType":"invoice","receivedDate":"2024-12-01"})

        Note: In real use cases, you can use variable to store these information instead, but you must always use ENCODE_JSON to convert them to JSON encoded text
        file

        Bind this value to formula using the following sample: MERGE([outputs["Take photo"].photoFile,{"mimeType":"image/jpeg"}])

        You will see some errors in the formula editor about unassignable object, you can safely ignore that.

        Note: This formula is needed due to an existing issue with missing mime-type in the Take photo output. Once the issue is fixed in the near future, you can simply bind this value to "Output of another node" > "Take photo / Photo file".


        Value for "options" key:

        Value for "file" key:

        Resulting list of key-values for the "Request body":
      5. Expand the Optional Inputs section of the "HTTP destination request":
        In "Path" set "/document/jobs" because that's the path to the API for uploading document to start an information extraction job. The path specified here will be appended to your destination in the final request.
      6. Your "HTTP destination request" is now ready.
    6. Drag and drop 1 "Toast" flow function to the Logic Canvas and link them to each of the output port 1 of the “HTTP destination request” flow function.
      For Toast linked to port 1 you can bind its message to formula with the following:
      "Result: " + outputs["HTTP destination request"].successStatusText
    7. Drag and drop 2 "Alert" flow function to the Logic Canvas and link them to each of the output ports of the “HTTP destination request” flow function.
      1. For the Alert linked to port 2, you can bind its message to formula with the following:
        "Error from backend: " + outputs["HTTP destination request"].failureStatusText
      2. For the Alert linked to port 3, you can bind its message to formula with the following:
        "Error: " + outputs["HTTP destination request"].error
    8. The logic canvas should looks like this:
    9. Save your project and you are ready to preview your app and test the file upload.

Preview and Trigger File Upload

"Take photo" currently only works in iOS or Android platform, therefore we will do the preview on your mobile devices.

  • On your mobile device, install the "SAP Build Apps Preview" app from Apple App Store or Google Play Store.
  • After the app is installed, launch the app on your mobile device and press the center card to create a login PIN code. You should see a temporary PIN code generated.
  • Go back to your Build Apps project in the browser and press on the "Preview" button
  • In the "Preview on your device" section, enter your temporary PIN code into the text box and press "Confirm pin". You should get "Mobile preview authenticated" message if it works.
  • Go back to the"SAP Build Apps Preview" app on your mobile device, you should see list of your apps that can be previewed.
  • Locate and open your app.
  • When the app is loaded, press the file upload button:
  • That should prompt you for permission to take photo (if this is the first time), approve it and take a photo of an invoice.
  • Once the photo is taken the upload via "HTTP destination request" should happen and you should get a result soon.
  • After successful upload you should get "Result: Created" toast:
  • If you check the Document Information Extraction web app, you should see your photo is now uploaded and queued for extraction.
  • Once the extraction is completed you should see be able to see it's result.

Summary

As summary, in this blog, you have learned how to set up your Build Apps project to make use of the "HTTP destination request" and "Take photo" to upload file using multipart/form-data payload.

If you are interested with uploading file to other services such as SAP Document Management Service using "Pick files" and "HTTP destination request" in SAP Build Apps, you can visit this blog: SAP Build Apps - upload files to SAP Document Management Service (DMS) repository with file picker

entry.title: SAP Build Apps - upload files to SAP Document Management Service (DMS) repository with file picker
entry.content:
Introduction

Uploading files is a common requirement in various business scenarios. As SAP Build Apps developer, we are often required to add file upload capability into our apps to fulfill business requirements and enhance user experience.

One of such use case is to upload documents to SAP Document Management Service (DMS) for storage purposes.

DMS Create Document API requires you to sent a POST request with "multipart/form-data" payload.

In this blog, I will show how you can make use of the new "HTTP Destination Request" flow function to achieve that in your SAP Build Apps project.

By the end of this blog, you will learn how to:

  1. Set up your DMS repository destination to work with Build Apps.
  2. Install your destination to be used in Build Apps and test the connection to your destination in Build Apps.
  3. Use "Take photo" to let user to capture photo with device and pass the resulting image to "HTTP destination request"
  4. Use "HTTP destination request" to upload the image to your DMS repository via the installed destination.

Pre-requisites

  1. You must have SAP Build Apps already subscribed in your subaccount.
  2. You must have SAP Document Management Service (DMS) subscribed with access to their API service.
  3. All required roles for SAP Builds Apps and Document Management Service are assigned accordingly to your user.

Setting up Destination for Document Management Service

If you already have a working destination to your DMS service, you can use it or clone it while referring to the steps below to make it compatible with the examples in this blog:

  1. Created a destination in SAP BTP pointing to your DMS instance.
    • For this example, I recommend that the URL of the destination is pointing to the /root API of your DMS instance:
      • Example: https://api-sdm-xyz.cfapps.eu10.hana.ondemand.com/browser/1234abcd-56ef-78ab-90cd-1234abcd567/root
      • You can always append additional relative path to your destination later when using the "HTTP Destination Request" flow function.
    • Add the required additional properties in the destination for Build Apps' usage:
      • HTML5.DynamicDestination: true
      • BuildApps.Enabled: true
      • BuildApps.ApiType: rest
      • MobileEnabled: true
    • These are the minimal properties required for Build Apps, you may add other properties for other purposes too. For example:

      Hint: "Check Connection" in the BTP Destination cockpit only tells you that the URL is reachable, it does not indicate that the authentication is set up correctly. So, you should not rely solely on it to tell you that your destination is configured correctly.

Installing DMS Destination in Your Build Apps Project

  1. Open your Build Apps project.
  2. Go to “Integrations” tab.
    1. Press on “Add Integration” button.
    2. Press on the “BTP DESTINATIONS” button in the SAP Systems card.
    3. Search for your DMS repository destination and select it. It should show a screen with “The destination was recognised as REST api” message.

      Note:
      • If you can't find your destination, do check that you have added the BuildApps.Enabled=true property in your destination.
      • If you don't see that message,do check that you have added the BuildApps.ApiType=rest property in your destination.
    4. Press "INSTALL INTEGRATION" button to install the destination.
    5. (Optional) Testing the connection to destination using Entity's list operation
      1. You can test the connection to your DOX destination by creating adding a REST API data entity and use it to call one of the GET API available in your service, assuming you have the right access. Otherwise, you can skip this steps or choose different API to test with.
      2. Press on the "Add REST API Data Entity" button. You should get a dialog pop up.
      3. In the dialog, enter the following information:
        • Name: Documents
        • Autogenerate configuration: enabled
        • Entity path within the API: /?cmisselector=children
        • Enable Capabilities: enable "list" and uncheck the rest of them
        • Press "Add" button
      4. You might see a warning about some troubles with the resource schema. You can safely ignore that as we are only using this as verification.
      5. You should now see the Documents entity and its Fields and more.
        If you are seeing other error here and it couldn't generate any Fields for you, then it's likely there's something wrong with your path, or destination configuration. Make sure to check your destination's URL and authentication configuration.
      6. You can use "Browse Real Data" button to check if you can see expected data entries and no errors.
      7. If you are not seeing any errors, it means the connection to your destination is good for usage.
    6. You can now Exit this page.

Using HTTP Destination Request to Upload File

  1. Go to “User Interface” tab.
  2. Press on the button (or any other UI control) that you wish to use to trigger the file upload and then press on the "Add logic to <your control>" at the bottom of the screen. This should show the Logic Canvas.
  3. Press the “Marketplace” button under the "Core" tab on the left panel of the Logic Canvas.
    1. Search for “Pick files” flow function and install it and then re-open the Marketplace again.
    2. Search for “HTTP Destination Request” flow function and install it.
    3. After both are installed you should see them in the "INSTALLED" tab of the Logic Canvas.
  4. You are now ready to use “HTTP destination request” to upload file:
    1. Go to the "INSTALLED" tab of the Logic Canvas.
    2. Drag “Pick files” to the logic flow canvas.
    3. Link the button's “Event: Component tap” node to the “Pick files” node.
    4. Drag “HTTP destination request” to the logic flow canvas.
    5. Link “Pick files” node’s output port 1 to the “HTTP destination request” node, because we want to pass the files selected by user to the “HTTP destination request” node.
    6. Click on the “HTTP destination request” node and on its property panel.
      1. In the Destination dropdown select your installed destination.
      2. Set the “HTTP method” as “POST”.
      3. Set the “Request body type” as “multipart/form-data”.
      4. Set the “Request body” and add entries for each part needed for the target API. In this case, we will be using the DMS Create Document API which requires 10 properties:
        KeyValue
        cmisactioncreateDocument
        propertyId[0]cmis:name
        propertyValue[0]Bind this to formula using the following: outputs[“Pick “files].files[0].name
        propertyId[1]cmis:objectTypeId
        propertyValue[1]cmis:document
        filenamebind this to formula using the following: outputs[“Pick “files].files[0].name
        _charset_UTF-8
        includeAllowableActionstrue
        succinctfalse
        media bind this to formula using the following: outputs[“Pick “files].files[0]
        Note: “Pick files” flow function allows user to pick one or more files. In this scenario, we will only take the first item of the selected files by accessing index 0, hence the files[0]
        For example:
      5. Your "HTTP destination request" is now ready.
      6. ℹ️ Optional Inputs
        In the Optional Inputs section of the"HTTP destination request", you can also specify additional "Path" that’s relative to your destination, if needed. The path specified here will be appended to your destination in the final request. In this example, we don’t need to specify path as our destination is already pointing to the target API path. However, for DMS create document API you may append inner folder path, if you wish, e.g. /FolderOne
        You can also specify additional HTTP "Headers" here.
    7. Drag and drop 1 "Toast" flow function to the Logic Canvas and link them to each of the output port 1 of the “HTTP destination request” flow function.
      For Toast linked to port 1 you can bind its message to formula with the following:
      "Result: " + outputs["HTTP destination request"].successStatusText
    8. Drag and drop 2 "Alert" flow function to the Logic Canvas and link them to each of the output ports of the “HTTP destination request” flow function.
      1. For the Alert linked to port 2, you can bind its message to formula with the following:
        "Error from backend: " + outputs["HTTP destination request"].failureStatusText
      2. For the Alert linked to port 3, you can bind its message to formula with the following:
        "Error: " + outputs["HTTP destination request"].error
    9. The logic canvas should looks like this:
    10. Save your project and you are ready to preview your app and test the file upload.

Preview and Trigger File Upload

  • Press on the "Preview" button > Open web preview and choose your app.
  • When the app is loaded, press the file upload button:
  • Pick a file from your file system and press Open:
  • After succesful upload you should get "Result: Created" toast:
  • You should see the file now uploaded to your target repository.
  • If possible, check your repository for the uploaded file.

Summary

As summary, in this blog, you have learned how to set up your Build Apps project to make use of the "HTTP destination request" and "Pick files" to upload file using multipart/form-data.

If you are interested with uploading file to other services such as SAP Document Information Extraction Service using "Take photo"and "HTTP destination request" in SAP Build Apps, you can visit this blog: SAP Build Apps - upload files to SAP Document Information Extraction (DOX) Service repository with camera.

entry.title: Authorizations for SAP Business Technology Plattform - a quick start
entry.content:
1. Introduction to Clean Core:

For SAP customers, hybrid authorization concepts are especially vital under the SAP clean core strategy. With this approach the aim is to keep the core SAP systems lean, minimizing customizations for easier maintenance and updates: 

Having a closer look into the so-called side – by – side extensions, we recognize the following typical architecture: 

Business Data is transferred to SAP BTP (e.g. using SAP Smart Data Access – SAP SDA / SAP Smart Data Integration – SAP SDI) and stored in SAP HANA Cloud as part of a CAP Application running on SAP Business Technology Platform. 

Such an architecture requires protection on two different levels: 

1. Structuring the access to the apps via SAP Build Workzone Standard Edition Roles 

2. Restricting the access on business data level (e.g. display / change, restrict to dedicated company codes, plants, …) 

In this blogpost, we will focus on the second restriction level. 

2. Overview of SAP BTP Levels and their Authorization Management 

To understand the authorization management on SAP BTP, it is important to know the setup of the platform and its levels. When managing authorization on the levels, each is handled separately, and permissions are granted independently. For example, a role on a global account level will not be available in subaccounts. 

On each level below, predefined role collections can be granted. A role collection is a set of individual roles that includes permissions for various resources and services on SAP BTP. It can contain one or multiple roles. 

Global Account: 

The Global Account is the highest level in SAP BTP's organizational structure. 

It represents the contractual boundary of your SAP BTP subscription. On this level it is possible to create custom role collections besides the predefined role collections. 

Directory: 

Directories can optionally be used to group subaccounts for better organization and shared resource management. 

Subaccount: 

Subaccounts are created within the Global Account and are used to manage entitlements, quotas, and assign services specific to each subaccount. On this level it is also possible to create custom role collections besides the predefined role collections. The subaccount level is the most important level for authorization management, as you can create custom roles to protect business applications.  

Org (Organization): 

In the context of Cloud Foundry environments, an Org is used to manage and isolate resources. 

Space: 

Spaces are created within Orgs. It is a logical container that is used for app development, deployment and maintenance. 

3. Authorization Management on the Subaccount Level – Creating a custom role  

Creating custom roles for business applications at the subaccount level requires interaction across three domains. Here’s a step-by-step guide to help you understand this process: 

3.1 Provision Role Templates by SAP BTP Developer (grey box) 

The first step involves setting up role templates, which are provided by the SAP BTP Developer. These templates can be tailored within the customer’s business application based on existing attributes and scope. 

  • Scope: Defines the actions an app needs to execute specific functions, such as editing, viewing, or deleting. 
  • Attributes: Used to specify static or instance-based authorizations that vary depending on the context, such as location, department, or cost center. Attributes help refine business users' authorizations, particularly concerning organizational levels like plants or company codes. 

3.2 User Account and Authentication (UAA) Level managed by SAP BTP Authorization Administrator (blue box)

At this stage, role templates transform into roles and role collections. Here's how it works: 

  • A role directly corresponds to a created role template. 
  • A single role template can inform multiple roles by creating roles with different attributes. 
  • These roles need to be assigned to one or more role collections. A role collection can encompass multiple roles, enhancing flexibility in user permissions. 

3.3 Assigning Roles to Users by User Administrator (green box) 

  • Direct Assignment: Assign one or more role collections directly to users on the User Account and Authentication level. 
  • Group Assignment: Assign one or more role collections to user groups. Note that roles cannot be directly assigned to individual users or user groups. 

By following these steps, you can effectively create and manage custom roles for business applications, ensuring that users have the appropriate permissions to perform their tasks efficiently. 

4. Quick Start Service and Tool Support (by SAP Consulting)

The role concept for SAP Business Technology Platform can become complex quickly, if business data is stored as part of a CAP Application. Just let us validate the following (fictive) example: 

A medium / large size customer plans to cover his plant maintenance processes via a clean core approach and requires three extensions which are developed on SAP Business Technology Platform. All three applications have a simple authorization concept consisting of an admin role, an expert role and a display role, so we have overall 9 functional roles available. Decentralized access is required for 20 plants, so the security team has to manage 180 BTP roles. 

The SAP Service provides the following features: 

  • Knowledge Transfer Workshop for BTP Authorizations 

Learn how the Authorizations on SAP BTP are managed. Get a Deep Dive on the BTP Levels, Role Collections and Roles and Role Templates.  

  • A best practice basis role concept for a quick start of the SAP BTP development project 

The customer can utilize the best practice role templates provided by SAP to get a quick start on their project. This will help them quickly structure their role management for BTP operations, BTP security, BTP developer and tester. 

  • AIRC BTP Role Collection Generator 

- UI5 User Interface: 

The UI5 interface will provide the customer with an user-friendly way to manage and track their roles and role collections, making it easier for the authorization team to monitor and adjust access controls as needed. 

- Download Roles and Role Collections via Excel: 

The customer’s security team can use this feature to export their role and role collection configurations into Excel. This is beneficial for documentation, audits, and for backup purposes.

- Update Roles and Role Collections via Excel: 

With this feature, the customer will be able to make bulk updates to their roles and role collections through Excel. This is useful for managing changes efficiently, especially when adjustments need to be made across multiple roles or role collections.

- Transfer Roles and Role Collections via Excel across subaccounts: 

With this feature it is possible to transport roles and role collections across the system landscape (dev, qas, prd) as long there is no integration into transport management. Another use case is to transfer your basis role concept across different subaccounts.

If you are interested in this service, please get in contact with the authors of this blogpost.

5. Introduction to Authorization Management Service (AMS) 

Authorization Management Service (AMS) is a new, improved method to develop and manage user authorizations for SAP Business Technology Platform applications in a centralized way. This blogpost will not further focus on details of this service, but conclude with the general recommendations which technology to choose in which situation: 

  • For new developments, the usage of AMS is recommended 
  • It is not mandatory to update existing developments to AMS. However, using AMS can benefit your overall authorization management posture in BTP and help manage BTP authorizations more effectively. Unfortunately, there is no automatic migration to AMS possible so that developments must be adapted, and policies must be created and distributed “manually”. 

Currently, the Role Collection Generator just covers the classical approach. With the availability of corresponding interfaces, the role collection generator will also cover AMS. 

The following source provide some further information on AMS: 

  • Configuring Authorization Policies 
  • Assign Authorization Policies 
  • AMS in the CAP “Golden Path” documentation 

6. Wrapping it up – What are the key takeaways? 

Hybrid authorizations are crucial for SAP solution extensions in the context of the SAP clean core strategy. SAP BTP involves multiple levels where authorizations are managed separately. Custom roles and role collections can be created and assigned at the subaccount level, ensuring appropriate user permissions. Tools like the AIRC Role Collection Generator and Authorization Management Service streamline role management and enhance security. If you want to learn more about SAP S/4HANA authorization concepts, please visit our first blog.

entry.title: Event-Driven Multi-Tenant Architecture on SAP BTP with CAP and SAP Event Mesh
entry.content:
Introduction

One of the key fundamentals of cloud native applications is that they are not monolithic, which means they are composed by a series of microservices – each one performing a very specific task – and those microservices are loosely-coupled - meaning they essentially do not depend on each other to achieve their outcomes.

A common technique to achieve loosely-coupling is to make microservices communicate with each other through what’s called event messaging: an event that is triggered by one service – e.g. creation or updating of a service entity – generates a message that is “published” (or posted if you will) to an “event message broker” which, then, stores it in a “message queue” to be consumed by another services. Such message contains relevant information about the event – e.g. the unique identifier of the entity for which the event was triggered.

An example of open-source message broker is Apache Kafka which handles messages that follow the CloudEvents specification.

SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) offers four different message broker services: SAP Event Mesh, SAP Event Mesh on Integration Suite, SAP Integration Suite, Advanced Event Mesh and SAP Cloud Application Event Hub. These services also implement the CloudEvents specification. The choice for one of them depends mostly on the characteristics of each use case where event messaging is required.

The Business Problem

Although those services are quite robust, reliable, and resilient in compliance with cutting-edge cloud application standards, they do not offer the capability of being consumed by multi-tenant applications, which means they are not “tenant-aware” like the destination and the authorization and trust management (XSUAA) services, among others.

For a better understanding of the issue, let’s picture the following business scenario: imagine you have two different customers running on different S/4HANA Cloud instances – let’s call them S4-1 and S4-2 - which are publishing event messages (called topics) to one of the message broker services instantiated in a subaccount on SAP BTP.

Now, let’s suppose that same subaccount serves as provider of a multi-tenant application.

Then, you create two different subscriptions of such multi-tenant application – each one in its corresponding consumer subaccount - and you want the first subscription to receive messages from S4-1 and the second from S4-2 – like you do when a subscription consumes APIs from S4-1 and another from S4-2 via the destination service.

As the message brokers from BTP are not tenant-aware - like the destination service -, that scenario is not possible by default.

The Solution

The fact that you have services which are not tenant-aware doesn’t mean that, with some creativity, you cannot overcome such obstacle. You can solve such kind of situation by simply putting a service in-between the non-tenant-aware service and the multi-tenant application and make such service tenant-aware.

A tenant-aware service requires some kind of “tenant registration” to control which tenant should be served on each request. That’s basically the “backbone” of a tenant-aware service, which is then enriched with other multi-tenant capabilities.

In our case we will develop an “event message dispatcher” service which will act as a kind of “hub” that consumes the messages published by “n” different S/4HANA Cloud tenants to a message queue of a client and then, distribute (dispatch) them to their corresponding subscriber (consumer) tenant.

Well, you might ask: “how do I tie an S/4HANA Cloud tenant to a subscriber tenant?”. Simple: if you inspect the CloudEvents specification, you’ll notice that every message must contain the so-called “context attributes”, one of them being the “source” of the event. That attribute must be unique for each message context, meaning that each S/4HANA Cloud tenant has a unique id for messages published when an event is triggered by an action performed on a BO - such Business Partner (e.g. BP created, or BP changed). For example: when a Business Partner is created on S4-1 the event “source” will be different than the “source” of a Business Partner created on S4-2, but always the same for the Business Partner BO (e.g. S4-1 issues source “/default/sap.s4.beh/740613139” whenever a BP is created or changed, whereas S4-2 issues source “/default/sap.s4.beh/740740843” for those same events).

Therefore, we use the source attribute of the message context to tie each S/4HANA Cloud tenant to a corresponding subscriber tenant in a BTP subaccount.

As previously mentioned, a tenant-aware service must provide some kind of “tenant registration” mechanism to control which tenant should be served on each request. In this specific case, the “request” is replaced by the message consumption from the message queue as it’s not coming from the subscriber, but, instead, being “pushed” to it. So, upon creation of a new subscription of the “multi-tenant event message consumer” application, a “registration” endpoint from the message dispatcher is called passing the relevant tenant information to subsequently tie it to a message source. The dispatcher, then, stores that information in its database and uses it to control the distribution of messages to their corresponding tenants according to the message source.

Solution Architecture

Let’s do a deep dive into the solution architecture for a better understanding of the big picture:

  1. An SAP Event Mesh service instance is created and configured in the provider subaccount. This represents the “message client” in which a message queue is created and “subscribed” to message topics from S/4HANA Cloud. Each S/4HANA Cloud tenant is configured to publish topics to the same message client using its “service key” to setup a communication arrangement.
  2. When the messages are received by the message client, they are stored in the subscribed message queue and then, consumed by the event message dispatcher service (which is a CAP Node.js service).
  3. A consumer subaccount creates a subscription of the event message consumer app (which is also a CAP Node.js service with a Fiori Elements UI).
  4. The SaaS Provisioning Service bound to the event message consumer app handles the subscription creation, whilst the Service Manager takes care of the tenant data isolation in SAP HANA Cloud through a specific database schema for each tenant.
  5. The SaaS Provisioning Service, then, invokes the “on subscription” callback endpoint from the event message consumer app which will, in turn, call the tenant registration endpoint from the event message dispatcher service via destination passing the relevant tenant information, which is stored in the dispatcher database (in this architecture, also SAP HANA Cloud). This communication is secured by the Authentication and Trust Management (XSUAA) service.
  6. When the event message dispatcher service consumes a message from the queue it takes the message source and uses it to search the registration database to find the corresponding tenant subscription. When the tenant is registered in the database the source is set to “new-tenant”, which means that this mechanism works on a “first-come-first-served” mode, hence if the dispatcher does not find the source in the database, it takes the first “new-tenant” source registered and ties the incoming source to it. Using the tenant information tied to the message source, the dispatcher calls a “consume endpoint” from the event message consumer app subscription, also via destination, passing the message data. This communication is also secured by the XSUAA service.
  7. When a user of the app accesses a subscription the Application Router is invoked using the subscription URL.
  8. The Application Router authenticates the user via XSUAA service. Authentication is performed by the configured Identity Provider of the subaccount with a SAML (or OpenID) response to XSUAA which, in turn, issues a JWT that’s forwarded to the event message consumer app. The app, after having processed the messages received from the dispatcher, presents the outcomes to the user via UI.

The Solution Prototype

We have developed a prototype implementing the reference architecture above to demonstrate how it works in practice and serve as a starting point for your own even-driven multi-tenant application. The prototype uses the “create” and “change” event message topics from the Business Partner BO, but, of course, the concept can be applied to other BOs as well.

The source code of the prototype is published to this Git Repo. There you’ll find detailed instructions on how to install and deploy it to your own BTP landscape.

The purpose of this blog post is not to go into the details of the code implementation, so, please, feel free to explore the source code as you wish. It’s fully commented and kind of self-explanatory.

In this post we will focus only on the key aspects of the destinations used in the implementation, because it’s there where the real magic lies.

Solution Destinations

The first one is the Event-Dispatcher destination, which is used by the event message consumer app to perform tenant registration:

  1. After you have deployed the event message dispatcher service to Cloud Foundry, you get the service URL from the deployed application and put it here.
  2. Select the XSUAA service binding of the application, view the sensitive data (service key), get the client id and client secret, and put them here.
  3. From the same service key get the authentication URL, append “/oauth/token” to it and put it here.

There is no mystery in this one: as the service is secured by XSUAA we just use the client credentials and the token service URL to authenticate to the registration endpoint and post the tenant data to it. So, as you may have seen in the Git Repo instructions you must first deploy the event message dispatcher service and then create the above destination.

The trick is in the second destination: Event-Consumer used by the event message dispatcher service to dispatch the message to the event message consumer app subscription.

  1. After you have deployed the event message consumer app to Cloud Foundry, you get the service URL from the deployed application and put it here. However, this one will be dynamically replaced by the event message dispatcher service with the tenant URL that’s been provided upon tenant registration.
  2. Select the XSUAA service binding of the application, view the sensitive data (service key), get the client id and client secret, and put them here.
  3. From the same service key get the authentication URL, append “/oauth/token” to it and put it here, but this time, replace the “sub domain” part of it with the word “subDomain”. And here is where the trick lies: this word is also dynamically replaced by the tenant (subaccount) sub domain that’s provided upon tenant registration.

You might be wondering: “why is that? with URL and token service URL being dynamically replaced this destination serves exclusively to securely store the client credentials?”.

Well, it’s basically that… however, it’s necessary because the “tenant id” for subscription authentication and data isolation is encoded in the JWT issued by XSUAA. Therefore, if you keep the provider app URL in the URL and the provider sub domain in the token service URL you’ll be authenticating in the provider and not in the consumer, thus generating a JWT with the provider tenant id instead of the consumer. This would prevent the event message dispatcher to properly dispatch the message to the corresponding tenant and the event consumer app to properly store the information in the corresponding database schema.

Conclusion

After reading through this blog post (and possibly deploying and inspecting the prototype source code) you may have learnt what’s an event-driven cloud solution and how to implement an event-driven multi-tenant architecture.

We hope you have enjoyed the ride and can take advantage of these learnings in a near future.

Thanks for reading!

entry.title: SAP Dynamic Forms 2411 Released Features
entry.content:
Welcome to the release of SAP Dynamic Forms version 2411.   In this blog, we will introduce you to the new features and provide you with an update on the product roadmap.  

Roadmap 

Below is the latest updated product roadmap, you can also check back later on the SAP Digital Asset portal for updates. 

 New Features  

Let us look at some of the features we are introducing in this release. For a more detailed listing, please refer to the official help documentation of SAP Dynamic Forms on the SAP help portal.    

Key Points 

  • New Industry Form controls in Form Builder to capture Measuring Points and GPS location 
  • Support of Dynamic Forms for S/4HANA Service and Service and Asset Manager on Windows (Android and iOS already supported)  

Industry Form Controls for Measuring Points and GPS coordinates.  

  • Form Controls are elements that allow business users to enter fields in forms 
  • The Measurement Point control allows end users to take readings via on the mobile or web application 
  • GPS control allows end users to enter their location or get a pre-populated location needed for their work. 

 

Pre- and post-processing of GPS location and measuring points requires configuration from the backend 

Support of Dynamic Forms for Service and Asset Manager on Windows 3.0 

  • Form associated with work orders, operations, and suboperations will be downloaded to the device to be completed offline during work execution 

Support of Dynamic Forms for S/4HANA Service  

  • View and complete form instances associated with service orders and items online or offline.  

Support of Dynamic Forms for Maintenance Tasks Lists 

 Limitations 

Dynamic Forms support in Fiori Applications is delayed and is currently being evaluated for a patch release in Q1 2025.   For customers expected to go live with this feature, please reach out to us.    

  • F2071 - Find Maintenance Notification 
  • F2175 - Find Maintenance Order 
  • F5104A - Perform Maintenance Jobs 

Embedded support to provide direct and context aware access to support knowledge base and support channels.   This feature is also delayed and will be enabled in our first patch release in Q1 2025.   

  • Support the ability to automatically create a support incident from within the product. 
  • Usage metrics on SAP4Me now includes user counts

entry.title: Stop Guessing! Use Case with Conversational AI Agents in SAP Analytics Cloud
entry.content:
Introduction

Have you ever stared at your analytics dashboard, wishing it could do more—like answer your questions without having to solely search, filter, and drill into your data? Well, guess what – you can do that now!

In this blog, I’ll walk you through an exciting use case that leverages SAP AI Core with SAP’s AI Agent through the SAP Business Technology Platform (BTP) in the following order:

  1. ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case – Business View
  2. What are AI Agents?
  3. Why SAP AI Agents?
  4. ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case – Technical View
  5. How can you get your hands on this?

 

1. ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case – Business View

Imagine you’re a business user at a Gas Provider Company, exploring your Finance and Billing Dashboards. As you dive into the billing data, a critical insight catches your eye: late fees are unusually high in a region. This sparks concern about potential customer dissatisfaction and retention issues. Here’s where the power of AI shines. By leveraging a Conversational AI Agent directly within your SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) dashboard, you can uncover the underlying trends, identify contributing economic factors, gain actionable recommendations, and more – removing the need to “guess” your next steps.

Let me show you how a Gas Provider Company can use a Conversational AI Agent to accelerate decision-making and uncover deeper insights beyond the 4 walls of their Enterprise. Watch the 'Stop Guessing' Use Case Demo below!

As you saw in the video above, the business user was able to receive an output that combines their Enterprise data from the HANA database and External data from PDFs and websites. This blog will further showcase how this capability allows businesses to have insights beyond the data in their 4 walls resulting in realistic, reliable results to drive business decision making. In other words, customers are no longer limited in data and information. With AI Agents you have the possibility to leverage a multitude of tools thereby providing you with a competitive edge. In fact, Gartner has predicted that by 2028 one third of interactions with generative AI will use autonomous AI Agents to complete tasks (Source).

To further understand the value SAP AI Core Services bring with AI Agents, we must first understand what an AI Agent is.

2. What are AI Agents?

AI Agents are NOT chatbots, but rather they are autonomous applications powered by large language models (LLMs) that can integrate with applications and business processes to achieve complex goals. They can fall into two categories:

  1. Task Oriented – Agents can automate workflows and multi-step tasks by leveraging integrations such as with SAP Build Process Automation.
  2. Conversational – Enable natural, human-like conversations to help users gain deeper insights as seen in the use case.

 Agents achieve their goal by using multi-step planning, self-examination and reflection of work to propose improvements, tool usage, and multi-agent collaboration to split-up tasks and come up with a solution. Some of these concepts will be seen in more detail in the ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case -Technical View section.

For example, in the use case video, the Conversational AI Agent examined the prompt in multiple steps by calling on the data and tools. In parallel, it also performed reflection activities to continuously learn and improve. Although there was no multi-agent collaboration here, an example would be a specific scenario AI Agent such as triggering a workflow to automate PR creations for approved marketing promo campaigns or, perhaps, calling on another Conversational AI Agent that has PR information specific tools to achieve goals around answering PR related topics.

 Source: Agents is the most important BTP release this 2024

Source: AI108 TechEd Session Session Catalog - SAP TechEd 2024

 

3. Why SAP AI Agents?

The market for AI Agents is rapidly growing with innovations from OpenAI, Microsoft, Google and other contributors. What makes SAP AI Agents valuable?

  1. SAP AI Agents integrate your semantically rich SAP data and processes, ensuring that the meaning of your business is captured. In other words, SAP knows SAP the best as it can understand, integrate, and capture your data's Business Context (eg. Hierarchies, metadata, currency conversion, languages, security configuration) - providing meaning to the AI Agent to reduce hallucinations and removing the need of manual rebuild of your data’s business context.
  2. The AI Agent integrates with SAP AI Core services by collecting data and leveraging underlying large language model (LLM) within the AI Core. It is also leverages HANA's Vector Engine for Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG). This advanced setup minimizes hallucinations, delivering more accurate, reliable, and trusted results.
  3. By leveraging SAP AI Core services and other BTP offerings, this will allow for improved efficiency and collaboration across your integrated Intelligent Enterprise Portfolio. You can create enterprise-grade AI Agents tailored to your unique needs that integrate seamlessly with tools like HANA, OData applications, SAP Build Process Automation, and even external sources like websites and PDFs, while ensuring you are the decision maker at the center of it all. This ensures secure interactions with your business data, processes, and systems while adhering to SAP’s clean core principles.

 

4. ‘Stop Guessing!’ Use Case – Technical View

 

In this section, we will dive deeper in the Use Case and explore the use case architecture and witness a Lab Preview of the Project Agent Builder – the new approach to develop agent-based projects in BTP.

Use Case Architecture

In this use case, the SAP Analytics Cloud (SAC) dashboard is leveraging data from the HANA database that has been further transformed and modeled in SAP Datasphere. With SAP Datasphere's native connectivity to SAC, the data maintains it's Business Context and can be consumed in real-time analytics. In addition, the Conversational AI Agent has been embedded into SAC as a custom application widget via Business Application Studio (and can also be embedded via SAP Build Code).

 

 

The AI Agent leverages 3 tools in this use case: Semantically rich data from HANA, Financial PDF documents, and the Gas Providers' Supplier Website. If required more tools can be added beyond the 3, allowing you to have that flexibility to achieve your goal and leverage Enterprise data with External data.

When a question is asked to the Conversational AI Agent in SAC, it sends this to the LLM and grounds it using the HANA Vector Engine & RAG while searching the tools for the appropriate data to respond back with. In addition, it performs muti-step planning to search for information using the 3 assigned tools to produce an answer while continuously reflecting for improvement.

Project Agent Builder

Project Agent Builder is where you can create your Conversational Agent and define the various tools and integrations to achieve your goal. Below you will see a lab preview screenshot of the Project Agent Builder. Upon creating a new Agent, you must identify the following:

  1. Description of the Agent’s expertise and goal with instructions to illustrate the Agent’s behaviour.
  2. Maximum thinking or multi-step planning steps it must take before providing a response.
  3. Selection of the LLM provider, which in this case is OpenAI GPT4o.

 

 To better understand how you can leverage the Project Agent Builder and how it operates in this Use Case, check out the video below!

 

5. How can you get your hands on this?

 

AI Agents are planned to be released as a BTP Service in H1 2025.

You can additionally leverage this BTP service with Joule Studio to extend beyond the core and collaborate between multi-agents for scenarios. Further roadmap innovations will also allow you to extend your AI Agent with SAP Build Process Automation to automate tasks.

Check out the links below for our AI Roadmap in Q1 and Q2 2025.

Q1 2025 Roadmap - New Generative AI Tool Kit with SAP HANA Cloud: SAP Road Map Explorer

Q2 2025 Roadmap for AI Agents for Advanced Business Scenarios:  SAP Road Map Explorer

Source: AI108 TechEd Session Session Catalog - SAP TechEd 2024

 Further information on AI Agents with Joule Studio can be found here.

entry.title: S4 HANA Graph Workspace with LangChain
entry.content:
Introduction

In today's world, data is extremely valuable, and being able to understand and use large amounts of information gives organizations a big advantage. Graphs are one of the best tools to help with this. They arrange data in a way that makes it easier to understand, explore, and analyze. This blog explores how to use graphs with Hana DB and GenAI features.

Prerequisites

  • SAP AI Core access and anthropic--claude-3.5-sonnet model deployed.
  • SAP HANA DB access.

The Core Components of a Graph

NODE: The node in a graph, represents entities like people, places, organizations, or concepts.

RELATIONSHIPS: The connections between entities that define how they are related or interact with each other.

ATTRIBUTES: Additional information about entities or relationships that provide further context.

SCHEMA: A formal representation of the knowledge within a specific domain, defining the types of entities, relationships, and the rules governing their interrelations.

Through this blog we will see how we can use HANA DB and Large Language Models to implement Graph which can provide with the answer to questions asked using natural language.

  • Create HANA DB graph workspace.
  • Insert data and data in vector format.
  • Generate the cypher query which is supported by SAP HANA Graph Workspace
  • Execute the query and get the answer.

Part 1 : Create Graph Workspace and Upload Data

For detailed explanation please refer this tutorial: Create an SAP HANA Graph Workspace | SAP Tutorials

 

Step 1 : Create two tables for Nodes and Edges of the Graph.

 

CREATE TABLE "ERP_NODES"(
	"NODE_ID"  NVARCHAR(5000) NOT NULL,
	"DESCRIPTION" NVARCHAR(5000),
	"NAME" NVARCHAR(5000),
	"NODE_TYPE" NVARCHAR(5000),
	"NODE_VECTOR" REAL_VECTOR,
	PRIMARY KEY (
		"NODE_ID"
	)
);

CREATE TABLE "ERP_EDGES"(
	"EDGE_ID"  NVARCHAR(5000) NOT NULL,
	"SOURCE" NVARCHAR(5000) NOT NULL,
	"DESTINATION"NVARCHAR(5000) NOT NULL,
	"RELATIONSHIP_TYPE" NVARCHAR(5000),
	"EDGE_VECTOR" REAL_VECTOR,
	PRIMARY KEY (
		"EDGE_ID"
	)
);

 

 

Step 2 :  Create Graph Work space

 

ALTER TABLE "ERP_EDGES" ADD FOREIGN KEY ("SOURCE") REFERENCES "ERP_NODES" ("NODE_ID") ON UPDATE CASCADE ON DELETE CASCADE ENFORCED VALIDATED;

ALTER TABLE "ERP_EDGES" ADD FOREIGN KEY ( "DESTINATION" ) REFERENCES "ERP_NODES" ("NODE_ID") ON UPDATE CASCADE ON DELETE CASCADE ENFORCED VALIDATED;

CREATE GRAPH WORKSPACE "ERP"
  EDGE TABLE "ERP_EDGES"
    SOURCE COLUMN "SOURCE"
    TARGET COLUMN "DESTINATION"
    KEY COLUMN "EDGE_ID"
  VERTEX TABLE "ERP_NODES"
    KEY COLUMN "NODE_ID";

 

 

Step 3: Download the data from here

Let's consider the following graph example for our case.

 

This excel contains sheets per node and edge. Node (Entities) sheet depicts Node with NODE_ID and Description.  Edge (relationships) sheet depicts how two nodes are related to each other.

 

Step 4: Upload the data using following code.

CODE:

 

import pandas as pd
from hdbcli import dbapi
import uuid
from gen_ai_hub.proxy.langchain.init_models import init_embedding_model
from gen_ai_hub.proxy import GenAIHubProxyClient
import os
from langchain_community.callbacks import get_openai_callback
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_ID"] = "AICORE_CLIENT_ID"
os.environ["AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"] = "AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET"
os.environ["AICORE_AUTH_URL"] = "AICORE_AUTH_URL"
os.environ["AICORE_BASE_URL"] = "AICORE_BASE_URL"
os.environ["AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"] = "AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"
gen_ai_hub_proxy_client = GenAIHubProxyClient(
    base_url="AICORE_BASE_URL",
    auth_url="AICORE_AUTH_URL",
    client_id="AICORE_CLIENT_ID",
    client_secret="AICORE_CLIENT_SECRET",
    resource_group="AICORE_RESOURCE_GROUP"
)
embeddings = init_embedding_model('text-embedding-ada-002')
HANA_DB_ADDRESS = "HANA_DB_ADDRESS"
HANA_DB_PORT = "HANA_DB_PORT"
HANA_DB_USER = "HANA_DB_USER"
HANA_DB_PASSWORD ="HANA_DB_PASSWORD"
 
driver = dbapi.connect(
    address=HANA_DB_ADDRESS, 
    port=HANA_DB_PORT, 
    user=HANA_DB_USER, 
    password=HANA_DB_PASSWORD
)
cursor = driver.cursor()
# Read data from Excel
excel_file = "data_blog.xlsx"  # Replace with your Excel file path
 
customer_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Customer')
order_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Order')
product_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Product')
supplier_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Supplier')
category_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Category')
customer_order_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Customer_Order')
order_product_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Order_Product')
product_supplier_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Product_Supplier')
product_category_df = pd.read_excel(excel_file, sheet_name='Product_Category')

 
# Function to create nodes
def create_node(cursor, id, description,node_type, node_vector):
    query = 'INSERT INTO ERP_NODES (NODE_ID, DESCRIPTION, NAME, NODE_TYPE, NODE_VECTOR) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?))'
    cursor.execute(query, (id, description, description, node_type, node_vector))
 
# Function to create relationships
def create_relationship(cursor, id, source, destination, relationship_type, edge_vector):
    query = 'INSERT INTO ERP_EDGES (EDGE_ID, SOURCE, DESTINATION, RELATIONSHIP_TYPE, EDGE_VECTOR) VALUES (?, ?, ?, ?, TO_REAL_VECTOR(?))'
    cursor.execute(query, (id, source, destination, relationship_type,edge_vector))
 
# Create Customer nodes
for index, row in customer_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Customer",node_vector=node_vector)

# Create Order nodes
for index, row in order_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Order",node_vector=node_vector)
        

# Create Product nodes
for index, row in product_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Product",node_vector=node_vector)
        

# Create Supplier nodes
for index, row in supplier_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Supplier",node_vector=node_vector)
        
# Create Category nodes
for index, row in category_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['ID']) and pd.notna(row['Description']):
        node_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query(row['Description']))
        create_node(cursor=cursor, id=row['ID'], description=row['Description'], node_type="Category",node_vector=node_vector)

# Create relationships between Customer and Order
for index, row in customer_order_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['Customer_ID']) and pd.notna(row['Order_ID']):
        customer_places_order_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("CUSTOMER_PLACES_ORDER"))
        order_associated_to_customer_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("ORDER_ASSOCIATED_TO_CUSTOMER"))
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Customer_ID'], destination=row['Order_ID'], relationship_type="CUSTOMER_PLACES_ORDER", edge_vector=customer_places_order_edge_vector)
        create_relationship(cursor=cursor, id=str(uuid.uuid4()), source=row['Order_ID'], destination=row['Customer_ID'], relationship_type="ORDER_ASSOCIATED_TO_CUSTOMER", edge_vector=order_associated_to_customer_edge_vector)
        

# Create relationships between Order and Product
for index, row in order_product_df.iterrows():
    if pd.notna(row['Order_ID']) and pd.notna(row['Product_ID']):
        order_has_product_edge_vector = str(embeddings.embed_query("ORDER_HAS_PRODUCTS"))
